transcript.transcript: They had them. It was the first one, 1937. Two years and they ran them. They were 50 cents, huh? And they were 50 cents. Yeah. See, on the inside, you've got your dates and your offenses and your penalties and places for your judge. Yeah, and if you get more than five, I think it was five sedations, you can get your driver's license back for a year. And then for a year, you're still in jail. Now if they pull a driver's license check, they'll call it in to the dispatcher. And he has to go down to Boise. They have to check their files to do it. And this way, the officer stopped and he just checked their driver's license. If they had five written there, they were suspended. Just a note here. On the inside of the driver's license back in Mr. Johnson's day, there were areas where you could make a citation you filled in your name what the citation was this was permanently on the inside of the driver's license all that was needed for driver's license check or previous record on the subjects driving was to open the inside of the drivers license. You were saying down in the city they just had one day man and one night man? Yeah. These people worked seven days a week then? I imagine they did. My in-group. Seven days a week? Yeah. Sometimes 30 or 40 hours a day. I remember hearing Mike Burns tell about it. He took a taxi. He got a call from somewhere out in the outskirts. He took a taxi to the city with reimbursement for his taxi fare. The law officers had to take taxis or flag down a personal citizen then to get out to the scene. Yeah. Larger arrest rate than they do now? Oh I found it very much. Mike wrote to the night man. He had trouble with the kids, and he just came to the city and asked them to go home. They told him. Had a little more of us. They were moving to see the mass. Down to the home, they were serving. All had one. All had one. Two. Of course, the population wasn't anything like it is now. There will be a small break in the tape at this time. However, it will continue shortly here with Mr. Johnson talking about Farragut State Park, the Navy population they had out there, and their lack of getting allocation for extra troops to work this area, their accident scenes, and the ambulance runs that they did have to make back then, which we'll start shortly. Because everybody's American. That's One of the tough jobs we had was they didn't increase our appropriation for the sheriff at all. We had that doggone mess of it. We would drive a driving ambulance, we kept the ambulance down at the Sheriff's office. And every time we'd have a wreck out there, there was that dog gone marrow, 95. There was one ship coming and one going all the time. It was working three ships out there. And they'd have a bad wreck. And we'd have to wait in an ambulance. I drove it most of the time. Out through that crack. The ambulance then was under the Sheriff's Department? It was, yeah. Well, Harry Spain drove it for a while, too. Yeah, he drove part of the time. And then after that, The morticians took over the ambulance service, didn't they? The morticians? Yeah. They took it over. What was your wages? What did they run back then for? $150 a month. $150 a month. And that was in what 36 1940 You mentioned through the state then what was it for the state? I started in $100 a month. And they finally raised it to $150 when I got it to be a patrol. Years of making a patrol. And I went to work in the Sheriff's Office in 1941, I guess. $150 a month. It was big wages. Oh, big wages back then, I think now the scale for them is pretty close to $600 a month they get paid. I know the state now starts out at $610, I believe it is. And the sheriff's office, I think, starts out at just close to $600. It's $570 some. Now, when you worked for the port of entry, that was under the state police department. Like it was, I think it was about two, three years ago or something like that. They changed it now. But it's still state police, isn't it? No, it's just under the state, but it's no longer under the state police. You're no longer a patrolman. Well, I know you're not a patrolman, but they have point of entry officers. Right. I thought it was, it used to be, when I was working, it was state police. Yeah, they've still got it, but when I knew, I know Gene Betz that works out there. And before he worked, he was just regular trooper. Now they're no longer troopers, but they're still under him. They've changed it quite a bit. Out there, they could move them around. They could take one man from the port and they could just put him right out in a patrol car. And now they've changed that and everything. They've just got the port of entry people, and your waymasters, and your state police. They even give you a completely separate test for it now. All you're responsible for is weighing trucks. Well I thought when they put them on the patrol then that they had to take additional training. Hello? Hello, your Frank? You have the wrong number. Well, who was the chief? You mentioned you worked at the Coeur d'Alene City, who was the chief at that time? I mean, Mike, well, you didn't work for the police, did you? I didn't work for the city police. He worked for the city, but not for the police. I drove a truck, the street department. For, a month, I guess. Well, here we go again. Hello. I guess you have. Otherwise, you'd like the Sheriff's Department, outside of the fact that later on it got into all Supreme Court laws and the business. When I first went in there, I volunteer for night work and one thing and another. Also, when I first went there, we didn't have any night men, just the one night man. And he slept right there. And there were three, three nephews. See, we'd go to work at 8 o'clock in the morning and I have an hour off for supper. Then go back and work till 8 o'clock at night. Put in a fairly long day then. Put in a couple of hours a day. Besides the night calls, I have to get up in the middle of the night. Well, every Saturday night you worked most of the nights, didn't you? Oh, yeah, Saturday nights. But A man that was off from that evening shift. Then he'd take the calls. And the whole night. I mean, plenty of them, too. What was your biggest problem on your calls then? Having your bar? What was your biggest problem on your calls then? Having your bar? What was your biggest problem on your calls then, having your bar fights or? Boy, everything. Bad ricks. They did a lot more of the traffic than the state police practically does it all now. Not does it all, but so much more of it than they did then. Yeah, we handled a lot of the traffic. In other words, he was out across the county helping with an accident. Somebody over here was getting robbed. They just had to wait. And we ran them, by and large, five or six wrecks ahead of us on Saturday night. We got there beyond one wreck and got it. After we got the radios, well, they had a handoff. I don't know how long we were there. Okay. And we called in another one down here somewhere, sometimes another one. We used to have to wait until we got there. Well, now I know when there's a traffic accident, the officers have to go out and they have to make a rough sketch of it, make all the measurements, and make a traffic report of it. What kind of a report did you have to make on the traffic accidents that you had? You didn't make any reports for anything? Unless it was somebody killed or something like that. We didn't make any written reports on them at all. We were a busy Saturday night when all the guys are in the beer bars and half drunk, and there'd be three or four wrecks ahead of us. We'd have a hell of a time keeping track of them, much less making a record of them. At this time, Mr. Johnson became very tired. Also could not seem to recollect any more instance of any significance and everything that had occurred. This will conclude the tape on the interview with Mr. Clifford B. Johnson, A one-time sheriff in the 30s and 40s, Kootenai County, Idaho. A one-time ISP, state trooper, a one-time waymaster, Coeur d'Alene, Idaho.
